hero.title: My Process
hero.subtitle: Think. Make. Check. Repeat until the right answer surfaces.

approach.title: How I Approach My Work
approach.body: Forming hypotheses, building what is needed to test them, and letting real user feedback drive every decision. Lean UX gives me a framework for staying close to the user without losing momentum, and it keeps the whole team pointed at outcomes rather than deliverables.

diagram.alt: Lean UX Process Diagram showing the cycle of Think, Make, and Check with Lean UX at the center
diagram.caption: The Lean UX loop: Think, Make, and Check, repeating in short cycles until the right answer surfaces.

principles.heading: Lean UX, By Preference
principles.body: Lean UX is my preferred approach to product design. I'm drawn to its emphasis on rapid experimentation, continuous user feedback, and tight collaboration between designers, developers, and stakeholders. Building, measuring, and learning in short cycles helps me stay focused on real user needs rather than getting stuck in heavy documentation or upfront assumptions.

principles.heading: Adaptable By Design
principles.body: That said, I'm comfortable adapting to whatever methodology a team relies on. I've worked within Agile and Scrum environments, and I understand how Waterfall can still make sense for projects with fixed scopes or strict compliance requirements. What matters most to me is contributing effectively to the team's workflow, not defending a single way of working.

principles.heading: Tools, Not Rules
principles.body: At the end of the day, methodologies are tools, not rules. I care more about shipping thoughtful, user-centred work than following any one process by the book. Whether a team moves fast and iterates or plans carefully from the start, I'm ready to meet them where they are and bring the same level of craft and curiosity to the work.
